source_paper_title: Extension of Additive Valuations to General Valuations on the Existence of EFX
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Mahara_2024, title={Extension of Additive Valuations to General Valuations on the Existence of EFX}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0044}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0044}, number={2}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Mahara, Ryoga}, year={2024}, month=may, pages={1263–1277} }
context:
Page 2, Introduction (discussion of prior work and open status of EFX existence for general valuations)

Despite significant effort, we were not able to settle the question of whether an EFX allocation always exists (assuming all goods must be allocated), and leave it as an enigmatic open question.
It is not known whether EFX allocations always exist even when \(n = 3\) for general valuations.
title: Prove existence of complete EFX allocations for general valuations setting
background_latex:
Let there be a finite set of indivisible items \(M\) and a finite set of agents \(N=\{1,\dots,n\}\). Each agent \(i\in N\) has a valuation function \(v_i:2^M\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) satisfying:
1) Normalization: \(v_i(\varnothing)=0\).
2) Monotonicity: if \(S\subseteq T\subseteq M\), then \(v_i(S)\le v_i(T)\).
An allocation is an \(n\)-tuple \(X=(X_1,\dots,X_n)\) of pairwise-disjoint bundles \(X_i\subseteq M\). It is complete if \(\bigcup_{i\in N} X_i=M\).
For agents \(i,j\in N\), agent \(i\) \emph{envies} agent \(j\) under allocation \(X\) if \(v_i(X_i) < v_i(X_j)\).
An allocation \(X\) is \emph{envy-free up to any item (EFX)} if for every pair \(i,j\in N\) and every item \(g\in X_j\),
\[
 v_i(X_i) \ge v_i(X_j\setminus\{g\}).
\]
Equivalently, after removing any single item from \(j\)'s bundle, agent \(i\) does not prefer the remainder to her own bundle.
The central general-valuation setting imposes no further structure such as additivity, submodularity, or identical valuations beyond normalization and monotonicity.
problem_definition_latex:
Given any numbers of agents \(n\ge 1\), any finite item set \(M\), and any collection of normalized, monotone valuation functions \(\{v_i:2^M\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\}_{i\in N}\), determine whether there must exist a complete allocation \(X=(X_1,\dots,X_n)\) of \(M\) that is EFX, i.e., satisfies
\[
\forall i,j\in N,\ \forall g\in X_j:\quad v_i(X_i) \ge v_i(X_j\setminus\{g\}).
\]
In particular, resolve the existence question for general valuations when all goods must be allocated.

An allocation is an \(n\)-tuple \(X=(X_1,\dots,X_n)\) of pairwise-disjoint bundles \(X_i\subseteq M\). It is complete if \(\bigcup_{i\in N} X_i=M\).
For agents \(i,j\in N\), agent \(i\) \emph{envies} agent \(j\) under allocation \(X\) if \(v_i(X_i) < v_i(X_j)\).
An allocation \(X\) is \emph{envy-free up to any item (EFX)} if for every pair \(i,j\in N\) and every item \(g\in X_j\),

Given any numbers of agents \(n\ge 1\), any finite item set \(M\), and any collection of normalized, monotone valuation functions \(\{v_i:2^M\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\}_{i\in N}\), determine whether there must exist a complete allocation \(X=(X_1,\dots,X_n)\) of \(M\) that is EFX, i.e., satisfies
\[
\forall i,j\in N,\ \forall g\in X_j:\quad v_i(X_i) \ge v_i(X_j\setminus\{g\}).
\]
In particular, resolve the existence question for general valuations when all goods must be allocated.
location_in_paper: Page 2, Introduction (discussion of prior work and open status of EFX existence for general valuations)
open_problem_quote:
Despite significant effort, we were not able to settle the question of whether an EFX allocation always exists (assuming all goods must be allocated), and leave it as an enigmatic open question.
It is not known whether EFX allocations always exist even when \(n = 3\) for general valuations.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: A counterexample search is conceivable by enumerating small \((n,m)\) and restricting valuations to a finite parametric family (e.g., integer-valued monotone functions) and then solving a feasibility check for EFX allocations, but the space of monotone set functions is enormous and requires strong discretization assumptions to be computationally tractable.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can encode candidate hard instances (finite valuation tables with monotonicity constraints) and use a fitness signal based on solving an exact/ILP or SAT check for existence of an EFX allocation, enabling an evolutionary search for potential counterexamples or tight families. However, without additional structure, the search space is very large and success may depend on clever instance parameterizations and efficient feasibility solvers.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
Mahara (2021) framed the central question for general (normalized, monotone) valuations: whether a complete EFX allocation must always exist. Building on the champion-graph framework of Chaudhury–Garg–Mehlhorn and the “EFX with charity” line, Mahara extended several additive-valuation existence results to general valuations via potential-function arguments (lexicographic and a new partition-leximin potential). In particular, Mahara proved complete EFX existence for two-valuation-type profiles and for the small-excess regime \(m\le n+3\), and improved bounded-charity guarantees to \(|U|\le n-2\) unallocated goods while maintaining that no agent envies the unallocated set.

The universal existence conjecture for complete EFX under general monotone valuations has since been resolved negatively by the paper titled “A Counterexample to EFX; Agents, Items, Monotone Valuations; via SAT-Solving”, which constructs an explicit instance with \(n=3\) agents and \(m=8\) goods admitting no complete EFX allocation, and further shows broad families of counterexamples for all \(n\ge 3\) once \(m\) is sufficiently larger than \(n\) (even for submodular valuations). This pins down the sharpness of Mahara’s positive results in the small-\(m\) regime and clarifies that any general-valuation existence theorem must impose additional structure (on valuations, on item-agent incidence, or by allowing charity/approximation).

Subsequent work has therefore focused on delineating maximal domains where complete EFX remains guaranteed and on principled relaxations. On the positive side, complete EFX is shown to exist for substantial structured subclasses of monotone valuations, notably multigraph/bipartite-multigraph valuation models where each good is relevant to at most two agents, and for leveled valuations where value strictly increases with bundle size. Complementarily, relaxations such as epistemic EFX (EEFX) restore unconditional existence for arbitrary monotone valuations, while other lines (e.g., rainbow-cycle-number methods) yield near-optimal \((1-\varepsilon)\)-EFX with bounded charity and additional welfare guarantees. Together, these results map a post-counterexample landscape: exact complete EFX fails in full generality, but remains attainable in rich structured settings and via carefully designed relaxations.

sources.title: A Counterexample to EFX; Agents, Items, Monotone Valuations; via SAT-Solving
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves non-existence of complete EFX allocations in the general monotone-valuation setting by exhibiting an explicit counterexample with 3 agents and 8 goods (monotone valuations) for which no allocation 
\(X=(X_1,X_2,X_3)\) satisfies EFX, and extends this to show: for every \(n\ge 3\) and \(m\ge n+5\), there exists an instance with submodular (hence monotone) valuations with no EFX allocation (Theorem 2). It also proves universal existence for the boundary case of 3 agents and 7 goods (Theorem 1).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether for arbitrary normalized, monotone valuations there must always exist a complete EFX allocation. This paper answers the existence question in the negative by constructing monotone valuations (already for \(n=3, m=8\)) with no EFX allocation, and further shows such counterexamples exist broadly (\(n\ge 3, m\ge n+5\)), even within submodular valuations. Thus it refutes the conjectured universal existence in the general-valuation setting (and a fortiori in the monotone setting).

sources.title: EFX: a simpler approach and an (almost) optimal guarantee via rainbow cycle number
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves two main results about EFX. (1) For exact EFX with complete allocation: with three agents, a complete EFX allocation exists provided at least one agent’s valuation is MMS-feasible (in particular, additive); the other agents may have arbitrary normalized monotone valuations (Theorems 1–2). (2) For approximate EFX with charity: the rainbow cycle number satisfies \(R(d)=O(d\log d)\) (Theorem 3/10), implying a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm that outputs a partial \((1-\varepsilon)\)-EFX allocation leaving \(\tilde O((n/\varepsilon)^{1/2})\) goods unallocated and with Nash-welfare guarantee (Theorem 4).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for existence of complete EFX allocations for arbitrary numbers of agents under general (normalized, monotone) valuations. This paper does not resolve that: it gives an exact-existence theorem only for \(n=3\) under a restriction (one agent MMS-feasible), leaving the fully general \(n=3\) monotone case open, and it does not address \(n\ge 4\) exact EFX existence. It also advances a different relaxation—\((1-\varepsilon)\)-EFX with bounded charity—by nearly tight bounds on rainbow cycle number, but this does not yield complete EFX for general valuations.

sources.title: EFX allocations and orientations on bipartite multi-graphs: A complete picture
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves existence (and algorithms) of complete EFX allocations for a restricted general-valuation model where each item is valued by at most two agents (a multi-graph instance). In particular:
- Theorem 4.11: For any fair-division instance whose valuation profile is representable by a bipartite multi-graph and where each agent’s valuation is normalized and monotone (with value depending only on incident edges), a complete EFX allocation always exists; it can be computed in pseudo-polynomial time (and in polynomial time for cancelable valuations).
- Theorem 5.1 / Corollary 5.2: For multi-cycles, EFX allocations exist for MMS-feasible valuations; for cycles with at least four agents, existence holds for monotone valuations as well (pseudo-polynomial time; polynomial for cancelable).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for existence of complete EFX allocations for arbitrary normalized, monotone valuations on all subsets of goods (no structural restrictions). This paper does not resolve that general question. Instead it establishes existence in a significant special case: valuations induced by (bi)partite multi-graphs, i.e., each good has positive marginal value for at most two agents and each agent’s value for a bundle depends only on the subset of incident goods. Within that restricted domain, it gives unconditional existence and constructive algorithms (Theorem 4.11), and extends to multi-cycles (Theorem 5.1). Thus it is partial progress: it expands the largest known valuation classes guaranteeing EFX, but leaves the unrestricted general-valuation setting open (indeed it ends with a conjecture for general multi-graphs, still far from arbitrary valuations).

sources.title: On the existence of EFX allocations in multigraphs
sources.theorem_or_result: In the multigraph valuation model (goods are edges; each agent/vertex values only incident edges; valuations are normalized and monotone), the paper proves existence of complete EFX allocations under any of the following structural conditions on the multigraph: (a) the multigraph is bipartite (Theorem 1), or (b) every vertex has at most \(\lceil n/4\rceil-1\) neighbors (Theorem 2), or (c) the shortest cycle consisting of non-parallel edges has length at least 6 (Theorem 3). In each case, all edges are allocated and the resulting allocation \(X\) satisfies \(\forall i,j\ \forall g\in X_j: v_i(X_i)\ge v_i(X_j\setminus\{g\})\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether complete EFX allocations always exist for completely general monotone valuations over an arbitrary item set (no structural restrictions). This paper does not resolve that question. Instead it proves existence in a restricted but nontrivial subclass of general monotone valuations: the (2,\(\infty\))-bounded/multigraph setting where each good is relevant to at most two agents (incident endpoints), and additionally requires one of three graph-structural conditions (bipartite, bounded neighborhood size, or large girth in the underlying simple graph of non-parallel edges). Thus it is partial progress toward the general-valuation existence question by expanding the known positive territory beyond simple graphs and beyond additive valuations in multigraphs.

sources.title: Fair and truthful allocations under leveled valuations
sources.theorem_or_result:
Theorem 4: For any number of agents and indivisible goods, if all agents have leveled valuation functions (normalized, monotone, and strictly increasing with bundle cardinality: 
- if \(|S|>|T|\) then \(v(S)>v(T)\)), then a complete EFX allocation exists. In particular, Algorithm 1 (a serial dictatorship with quotas \(\lfloor m/n\rfloor\) for \(n-r\) agents and \(\lfloor m/n\rfloor+1\) for \(r\) agents, where \(m=kn+r\)) outputs an allocation \(A\) such that \(\forall i,j\,\forall g\in A_j: v_i(A_i)\ge v_i(A_j\setminus\{g\})\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether complete EFX allocations always exist for completely general monotone valuations (no structure beyond normalization and monotonicity). This paper proves existence of complete EFX allocations only for the restricted class of leveled valuations, which is a strict subclass of monotone valuations (it imposes strict preference for larger cardinality bundles regardless of item identities). Thus it does not resolve the general-valuation existence question, but provides a positive existence theorem in a non-additive, non-submodular setting that still allows arbitrary rankings among equal-size bundles. The proof/algorithm (quota-based serial dictatorship) leverages the cardinality-driven structure; it does not extend to arbitrary monotone valuations where bundle size alone does not determine strict preference.

sources.title: Epistemic EFX allocations exist for monotone valuations
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves existence of epistemic EFX (EEFX) allocations for any number of agents with normalized, monotone (general) valuations: for every instance 
I=([n],M,(v_1,\dots,v_n))
 there exists a complete allocation \(X=(X_1,\dots,X_n)\) such that for each agent \(i\) there exists a partition of \(M\setminus X_i\) into \(n-1\) bundles \(C_1,\dots,C_{n-1}\) with \(v_i(X_i)\ge v_i(C_j\setminus\{g\})\) for all \(j\) and all \(g\in C_j\) (Theorem 3.5). It also shows computing EEFX is hard: exponential value-query lower bound and PLS-hardness even for identical submodular valuations (Theorems 4.7, 4.8).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for existence of complete EFX allocations under general monotone valuations. This paper does not prove EFX existence; instead it proves existence of a relaxation, EEFX, for the full generality of monotone valuations. Thus it provides progress only in the sense of identifying a fairness notion close to EFX with unconditional existence, and it highlights that computational hardness for EEFX parallels known hardness for EFX. It does not resolve whether EFX itself always exists when all goods must be allocated.
